title: Tarka dal
subtitle: Red lentils with a spiced butter and cumin tarka.
excerpt: Red lentils simmered with turmeric, tomato and ginger until thick and silky, then finished with a hot tarka of ghee, cumin seeds, mustard seeds and dried chilli poured sizzling over the top. Serves four as a side, two as a main.
sourceNotes: Tarka dal. The dish is one of the foundational preparations of the Indian subcontinent, eaten across India, Pakistan and Bangladesh. Tarka (also tadka or chaunk) refers to the finishing technique of pouring hot spiced fat over the cooked lentils. Madhur Jaffrey's Indian Cookery (1982) provides the primary English-language reference for this technique.

recipe.yieldDescription: 4 as a side, 2 as a main
recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the dal base without the tarka for up to two months. Make fresh tarka when reheating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dal base keeps in the fridge for four days. Make tarka fresh when serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: Also tadka or chaunk. A finishing technique from Indian cooking in which whole spices are briefly fried in hot fat until they pop and release their fragrance, then poured over a dish. The hot spiced fat blooms the flavours in a way that cooking the spices from the start does not.

content.text: The lentil base is simple; the tarka is what makes the dish. When ghee hits the cumin and mustard seeds at the right temperature, they pop and release a burst of fragrance that transforms the bowl of lentils below. Do the tarka last, when everything else is ready, so the sizzle hits the dal at the table.

Heat {{rapeseed-oil}} of oil in a saucepan over medium heat. Cook {{onion}} diced onion for 8 minutes until golden. Add {{garlic}} of grated garlic and {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger; cook 2 minutes. Add {{turmeric}} of turmeric, stir, then add {{tomato}} chopped tomatoes. Cook for 5 minutes until broken down. Add {{red-lentils}} of rinsed lentils and 800 ml of water. Bring to a simmer; cook for 20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the lentils are soft and the dal is thick. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt.

Heat {{ghee}} of ghee in a small frying pan over medium-high heat until very hot. Add {{mustard-seeds-black}} of mustard seeds; when they start to pop, add {{cumin-seeds}} of cumin seeds and {{garlic}} of sliced garlic. Fry for 30 seconds until the garlic is golden. Add {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes and remove from the heat immediately. Pour the hot tarka over the dal.

items.cause: Garlic or spices overcooked in the hot ghee.
items.fix: The tarka takes under a minute from start to finish. Prepare all ingredients before heating the fat; work fast.

items.cause: Cooked too long without enough water.
items.fix: Add a splash of hot water to loosen. Red lentils absorb a lot of water; check and stir every 5 minutes.

items.cause: Insufficient onion caramelisation, or tarka not sizzling hot enough.
items.fix: The onion needs a full 8 minutes to develop sweetness. The ghee for the tarka must be smoking-hot before the seeds go in.

content.text: Dal in some form is eaten daily across the Indian subcontinent, from the simplest lentils with turmeric to the elaborate dal makhani of Punjabi restaurants. Tarka dal sits in the everyday middle: easy to make, cheap, nourishing, infinitely variable with whatever lentils or aromatics are to hand. Serve with basmati rice and a flatbread, or alongside a meat curry as a vegetable element.
